title: Introduction to Rust Programming for Social Work Systems
description: Learn Rust fundamentals through building secure, reliable systems for social work case management and client data protection.

nodes.title: Programming Background Quiz
nodes.content: Answer these questions to determine your learning path. This diagnostic will help us tailor the course to your programming experience level.

questions.question: You're designing a client database for a social services agency. Which statement best describes what a variable is?
options: A named container that holds a value, like a case file folder holding client information
A command that runs a program
A type of error in code
A visual element in user interfaces
feedback: Correct! Variables store data just like case files store information.
Not quite. Variables are used to store data, not execute commands.
Variables are programming concepts, not errors.
Variables are data containers, not UI elements.
questions.explanation: Variables are fundamental to programming—they hold data that your program uses, similar to how case files hold client information in social work.

questions.question: In a system tracking client caseloads, what does 'memory safety' mean in programming?
options: Using a lot of RAM to store data quickly
Protecting data from being accessed or modified incorrectly, preventing security breaches
Making sure backups are stored safely
Encrypting all data on disk
feedback: That's RAM performance, not memory safety.
Exactly! Memory safety prevents unauthorized access and corruption of sensitive client data.
Backups are important but not what memory safety means.
Encryption is one security tool, but memory safety is broader.
questions.explanation: Memory safety ensures data integrity and prevents unauthorized access—critical when handling confidential client information in social work systems.

questions.question: Which programming concept helps prevent errors like accidentally overwriting a client's record?
options: Comments
Type systems that enforce what kinds of data can be stored
Comments in documentation
Cloud storage
feedback: Comments document code but don't prevent errors.
Correct! Strong type systems prevent many categories of errors.
This is similar to option A.
Storage location doesn't prevent data errors.
questions.explanation: Type systems act like data validation rules, ensuring client data maintains its integrity throughout the system.

nodes.title: Rust's Memory Safety & Ownership Model
nodes.content: Rust's ownership system is revolutionary for building secure social work systems. In Rust, every value has exactly one owner—similar to how each case file has a designated responsible caseworker. This prevents two handlers from accidentally corrupting the same client record. When data is passed to a function, ownership can be transferred (moved) or borrowed (temporarily accessed). This model eliminates entire categories of bugs that plague traditional systems handling sensitive information. Rust's compiler enforces these rules, catching errors at compile-time rather than letting vulnerabilities slip into production where they could expose client data.

nodes.title: Building a Secure Client Record System
nodes.content: Imagine a crisis intervention team building a system to track vulnerable clients. In Rust, you define a ClientRecord struct with fields like name, intake_date, and risk_level. When a social worker function needs to review the record, it can borrow it without taking ownership—the original record stays protected. If code attempts to access a field without proper authorization level, Rust's type system catches it. For example, a junior caseworker's function might borrow with read-only access, while a supervisor's function has mutable access. The compiler ensures this at build-time, preventing runtime security breaches that could expose vulnerable client information.

nodes.title: Congratulations: Advanced Path Complete
nodes.content: You've mastered Rust's core safety principles! You're ready to build production systems that protect sensitive client data. Consider exploring Rust's async/await for handling concurrent case management requests, or web frameworks like Axum for building secure social work APIs. Your strong programming background positions you to leverage Rust's unique advantages in systems that handle vulnerable populations' information.

nodes.title: What is Rust? Why Does It Matter for Social Work?
nodes.content: Rust is a programming language designed to prevent entire categories of errors—especially those involving memory and data corruption. For social work, this is critical: client records contain sensitive information about vulnerable people. Traditional languages allow subtle bugs that could leak confidential data or corrupt case histories. Rust's compiler catches these bugs before code runs. It's like having a safety checklist that prevents case files from being misplaced or accessed by unauthorized staff. Rust is used by major tech companies building systems where security is non-negotiable. Learning Rust equips you to build or audit systems that safely handle client information.

nodes.title: A Simple Client Data Handler in Rust
nodes.content: Let's build a simple example: You're creating a system to log when clients visit. You write let client_name = "Maria"; to create a variable holding a client's name. In Rust, once you create this variable, the compiler knows its type (text/String) and ensures it's used correctly. If another part of your code tries to treat client_name as a number, the compiler rejects it immediately. You can create a function fn check_in_client(name: String) that documents exactly what kind of data it needs. The compiler verifies every function call matches this contract. This prevents bugs where, for example, a visit log accidentally receives a date instead of a name, corrupting the record.

nodes.title: Practice: Building a Case Activity Logger
nodes.content: Now you'll apply these concepts. Your task: outline how to build a simple Rust program that logs case activities. Think about what data you need (client ID, activity type, timestamp, caseworker ID), what functions you'd write, and how Rust's type system helps prevent errors.

questions.question: You're logging a counseling session. Which Rust feature best ensures the session date isn't accidentally treated as a client ID?
options: Comments explaining what data is what
The type system requiring each variable to declare its data type explicitly
Storing all data in a single variable
Using cloud backup
feedback: Comments help humans understand code but don't prevent errors.
Exactly! Rust's type system prevents type mismatches at compile-time.
Mixing data types makes errors more likely.
Backup is for disaster recovery, not error prevention.
questions.explanation: Rust's strong typing catches type errors before your program runs, protecting data integrity in sensitive case records.

questions.question: A social worker's program reads a client file, modifies it, and saves it. To prevent accidentally losing the original file, Rust should:
options: Always delete the original after reading
Allow any code to modify data freely
Ensure only one handler controls the data at a time, preventing conflicts
Store everything in memory without saving
feedback: Deleting originals creates data loss risks.
Allowing free modification enables corruption.
Correct! Ownership ensures data integrity by preventing simultaneous conflicting access.
Not saving defeats the purpose of case files.
questions.explanation: Rust's ownership system ensures safe data handling—only one process modifies a file at once, preventing the data corruption risks that plague traditional systems.

questions.question: Your team is building a crisis hotline system. Why would Rust be better than a language without strong type safety?
options: It runs faster
It prevents a caller ID from being treated as a phone number, avoiding routing errors that could disconnect someone in crisis
It makes code easier to read
It requires less training
feedback: Speed isn't the main advantage for this scenario.
Exactly! Type safety prevents confusion between data types, ensuring calls route correctly to trained responders.
Readability is important but isn't the unique safety benefit.
Rust has a steeper learning curve, not easier training.
questions.explanation: In crisis systems, data type errors could have life-threatening consequences. Rust's compiler catches these errors before they reach production.

nodes.title: Completion: Rust Fundamentals for Social Work
nodes.content: Excellent work! You've learned Rust's core concepts through a social work lens. You understand why memory safety matters for protecting client data and how Rust's type system prevents bugs. Next steps: practice writing small Rust programs, explore how to build web APIs for case management, and consider how these principles apply to systems you use in social work. Rust's growing ecosystem includes many tools for building secure services—you're now equipped to understand and use them responsibly.
